coredata.dc:title: “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescence switches based on N,S-doped carbon dots: Facile hydrothermal growth, selective detection of Hg2+, and as a reversive probe for guanine
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Farid
Iqbal, Shahzad
Zhao, Long
Xiong, Hai
coredata.dc:description: Water contamination due to heavy metal ions has become a major environmental problem worldwide. In this work, “on-off-on” fluorescence switches comprising N,S-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) have been developed for selective recognition of Hg2+ and as reversive probes for guanine. N,S-CDs were synthesized in a facile one-step hydrothermal approach using citric acid and methionine as precursors. The synthesized N,S-CDs display fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima of 370/440 nm and a quantum yield of 32.5%. Under the variable pH (2–12), the fluorescent N,S-CDs with a linear range from 0.05 to 100 μM displayed selective discrimination for Hg2+ with the limit of 6.24 nM over several other cations, anions, and neutral analytes resulting in the quenching of fluorescence response. Furthermore, the addition of guanine at the LOD of 6.4 nM can restore N,S-CDs' fluorescence in a reversible manner. For this kind of fluorescence switch, its purposed applications on environmental samples are employed successfully to detect Hg2+ in tap water and river water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
Carbon dots
Selective discrimination
Mercury
Guanine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the synthesis of N,S-CDs
Preparation of samples for detection of Hg2+ by N,S-CDs
Effect of pH on optical properties of N,S-CDs
Analytical performance of N,S-CDs for detection of Hg2+ and guanine
Effect of pH on the recognition of Hg2+ and guanine
Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China ( KQJSCX20180328095517269 ) and the NTUT-SZU Joint Research Program (Grant No. 2021003 ) for the research grants.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HR-TEM image of N,S-CDS (B) Average size of N,S-CDs by intensity (C) d-spacing and (D) SAED pattern of N,S-CDs.
(A)Full XPS survey scan of N,S-CDs, (B) Elemental composition of N,S-CDs, (C–F) High-resolution XPS spectra C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S2p.
(A) Changes in the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs as a function of pH of the medium. (B) The number of cycles for pH detection using N, S-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence response of N,S-CDs upon addition of different metal cations (100 μM), (B) Selective recognition of Hg2+ in company of interfering metal cations and anions. 1 = K+, 2 = Na+, 3 = Ca2+, 4 = Mg2+, 5 = Ag+, 6 = Fe3+, 7 = Ni2+, 8 = Mn2+, 9 = Cu2+, 10 = Co2+, 11 = Pd2+, 12 = Pb2+, 13 = Cd2+, 14 = Cr3+,15 = PO4 3−, 16 = SO4 2−, 17 = NO3 1−, 18 = CO3 2−, 19 = CH3COO− and 20 = ClO4 −

(A) Change in N, S-CDs' fluorescence intensity upon adding different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.05–100 μM) (B) Linear plot of F/Fo versus concentration of Hg2+, (C) fluorescent turn-on response of N,S-CDs@Hg2+ complex with increasing concentration of guanine, (D) Straight line equation concerning guanine concentration at 440 nm. (F is the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs, and Fo is the fluorescent intensity of N,S-CDs + Hg2+).

Selectivity of N,S-CDs@Hg2+ for guanine in presence of similar analytes, 1 = Adenine, 2 = Alanine, 3 = Thymine, 4 = Cytosine, 5 = Glutathione, 6 = , Proline 7 = Lysine, 8 = Threonine, 9 = Ascorbic acid, 10 = Mixture.
Diagrammatic sketch for the synthesis and sensing applications of N,S-CDs.
Comparison of the current method with reported methods for the detection of Hg2+.
Results for the determination of Hg2+ in Tap water and River water samples.

head.ce:title: “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescence switches based on N,S-doped carbon dots: Facile hydrothermal growth, selective detection of Hg2+, and as a reversive probe for guanine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water contamination due to heavy metal ions has become a major environmental problem worldwide. In this work, “on-off-on” fluorescence switches comprising N,S-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) have been developed for selective recognition of Hg2+ and as reversive probes for guanine. N,S-CDs were synthesized in a facile one-step hydrothermal approach using citric acid and methionine as precursors. The synthesized N,S-CDs display fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima of 370/440 nm and a quantum yield of 32.5%. Under the variable pH (2–12), the fluorescent N,S-CDs with a linear range from 0.05 to 100 μM displayed selective discrimination for Hg2+ with the limit of 6.24 nM over several other cations, anions, and neutral analytes resulting in the quenching of fluorescence response. Furthermore, the addition of guanine at the LOD of 6.4 nM can restore N,S-CDs' fluorescence in a reversible manner. For this kind of fluorescence switch, its purposed applications on environmental samples are employed successfully to detect Hg2+ in tap water and river water.

ce:section.ce:para: Contamination of natural water reservoirs with toxic heavy metals poses a severe threat to aquatic and human's life through the food chain. Based on non-biodegradability and easy permeability through tissues, Hg2+ tends to accumulate into human bodies leading to the dis-function of vital organs [1–3]. So, it is crucial to develop a proficient approach for the determination of Hg2+ in water samples. Several analytical approaches for recognizing Hg2+ in ecological samples have been published in the literature [4–8]. However, these techniques require expensive instrumentation and elaborative sample preparation procedures.

Guanine is an integral part of the DNA construct, and its derivatives serve as biomarkers to monitor critical cellular processes, such as signal transduction. The aberrant level of guanine and its products indicates various health complications [9–11]. For instance, a higher level of guanosine is associated with carcinoma, and a lower level of guanosine monophosphate reflects neurodegenerative disease [12]. Similarly, an increased level of 8-oxoguanine indicates a risk of cancer-based on reduced DNA repair [13–15]. Although traditional analytical techniques (HPLC, gas chromatography, and mass spectrometry) effectively serve these tasks, these techniques are expensive and involve lengthy and hinder in-situ detection of the target analyte. Therefore, it is also critical to monitor in-situ selectively the concentration of guanine and its derivatives to prevent and diagnose different diseased conditions by developing rapid biosensing strategies.

Among the many analytical methods, sensors based on nanomaterials are gaining much interest nowadays due to the ease of their synthesis and selectivity [16,17]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have ascended as a competent material for various applications based on their optical properties, sensitivity towards various analytes, high stability, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and biocompatible [18–23]. Due to their fluorescence properties, carbon dots-based nanomaterial shows immense prospects in various fields such as sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, nanomedicine, etc. [24–26]. Furthermore, CDs doped with a heteroatom such as nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, etc., demonstrate enhanced fluorescence properties and can serve as fluorescent sensors for various environmental and biological analytes [27–29]. Recently, several fluorescent probes based on N,S-CDs have been reported for the selective detection of Hg2+ ions [30–32]. Hasan et al. recently published the synthesis of an N-CDs-based “on-off-on” probe for the detection of Hg2+ and glutathione with detection limits of 80 nM and 2000 nM, respectively [33]. The Zhuo group in 2020 reported the development of an N,S-CDs based “on-off-on” probe for the selective identification of Hg2+ and cysteine, with detection limits of 320 nM and 270 nM, respectively [34]. Nevertheless, most of the reported fluorescent probes based on N,S-CDs for Hg2+ have a narrow linear range or higher detection limit, restricting their use for practical applications.

Herein, we report selective and highly sensitive determination of Hg2+ and guanine in-situ using N,S-doped CDs. In this system, an ‘on-off-on’ fluorescence response was observed for the system of N,S-doped CDs/Hg2+ and the mixture of N,S-doped CDs/(Hg2+ + guanine) respectively under aqueous conditions. N,S-doped CDs are extremely discriminatory for detecting Hg2+ with a limit of 6.24 nM, and guanine at the LOD of 6.4 nM as no intervention was recorded from other similar analytes. In our “ON-OFF-ON” Fluorescence Switches, when the LOD of Hg2+ is one-twelfth and one-fiftieth times comparing with previous literature, and the LOD of guanine is one-three hundreds of tenth times than that of glutathione or one-fourth times than that of cysteine, respectively [ [33,34]]. Furthermore, this probe shows sensitivity towards change in pH and change in fluorescence response in-situ covering multiple cycles. These N,S-CDs show enhanced fluorescence response at basic pH and opposite quenched signal under acidic conditions. Furthermore, N,S-CDs based fluorescence sensor was effectively used for the recognition of Hg2+ in wastewater samples.

All chemicals and solvents were acquired with analytical purity and were utilized without any additional purification. Methionine and citric acid were bought from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China). CaCo3, CdCl2, CoCl2, CrCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, KCl, MgSO4, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, SmCl3, Tb(NO3)3, Zn(NO3)2, and NaOH were also acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co.,Ltd. Hg(ClO4)2 was purchased from Strem Chemicals, Inc. (United States). Adenine, Cytosine, Guanine, Thymine, Lysine, Serine, and ascorbic acid were also obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All the solution was prepared in deionized water.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) JEOL F-200 operating at 200 kV was used to determine N, S-CDs' particle size and surface morphology. The elemental composition was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo-VG Scientific ESCALab 250 microprobe equipment with a monochromatic Al K-alpha source (1361 ev). A Frontier FTIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) was utilized to record the FTIR spectra of N,S-CDs. The absorption spectrum was recorded using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs were recorded using an F-7000 spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the synthesis of N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-CDs were prepared by utilizing a conventional hydrothermal process with slight modifications to the previously reported methods [35]. In brief, 0.5 g methionine and 0.5 g citric acid were dissolved in 10 mL double distilled water and ultra-sonicated for 15 to make a clear solution. The resultant solution was then shifted to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave tube and warmed in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the resultant greenish suspension was diluted with 50 mL deionized water and subjected to centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min to separate the larger particles. The obtained solution was then dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h before being stored at 4 °C for future use.

The quantum yield of N,S-CDs was determined by the relative method using quinine sulphate as standard [36]. The quinine sulphate (0.1 M H2SO4) and N,S-CDs solution was prepared, and their absorbance was recorded. To minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance in the 1 cm fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.1. After that, the fluorescence intensity of the same solutions was recorded upon excitation at 360 nm, and quantum yield was calculated using the following equation.
Q
Y s
=
QY st
η
s 2
η st 2
Is As
A st
I st
Where QY is the quantum yield; η is the refractive index of the solvent; s represents the sample; st represents the standard; I is the fluorescence intensity, and A is the absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples for detection of Hg2+ by N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mercury (Hg2+) sensing experiments were conducted at 25ᵒC in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.0). For fluorescence experiments, carbon dots solution was synthesized by adding 10.0 mg of N,S-CDs into 50 mL of double-distilled water. In a standard Hg2+ detection experiment, 20 μL of N,S-CDs solution was mixed with 1 mL of buffer solution, then the measured concentration of Hg2+ solution was added. Once the solutions were combined, alteration of the fluorescence response was noted at room temperature, from 380 to 650 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. For quality control, each experiment for the calibration curve was repeated at least three times. The selectivity towards Hg2+ was determined through competitive experiments in the presence of several metal cations, anions, and neutral analytes. A typical competitive experiment solution of N,S-CDs and various analytes (100 μM) was added with Hg2+ (100 μM), and alterations in the fluorescence were noted. The influence of pH on N,S-CDs' fluorescence intensity, and selective detection of Hg2+ were also examined at a broader pH range (2–12). The aqueous solutions of 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH were utilized to adjust the pH level.

The application of N,S-CDs for recognizing Hg2+ in real water samples obtained from various sources (laboratory tap water and pearl river water) was also investigated. A different known concentration of Hg2+(50, 100, and 500 nM) were spiked in real water and treated with N,S-CDs. The variations in the fluorescence were logged to assess the effect of the matrix on selective recognition of Hg2+.

In the preparation of N,S-CDs, citric acid serves as a carbon foundation, while methionine serves as a functional doping agent for nitrogen and sulfur atoms (Scheme 1 ). In the hydrothermal process, N,S-CDs formation has been thought to be a complicated process involving many phases such as polymerization, aromatization, carbonization, and dehydration. Freshly prepared N,S-CDs are light green under sunlight, translucent, well dispersed, and water-soluble.

The following parameters were optimized to synthesize N,S-CDs-based fluorescent probe, including the ratio of citric acid (CA) and methionine (Meth) called CA/Meth, reaction temperature, and reaction time. The CA/Meth composition ratio significantly impacted the fluorescence and emission wavelength of N,S-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S1, CDs exhibited a sharp emission band around 440 nm with maximum fluorescence intensity at a 1:1 (CA/Meth) ratio. Upon increasing the ratio of citric acid 2:1 (CA/Meth) the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly. In contrast, increased methionine 1:2 (CA/Meth) concentration resulted in a redshift in the emission spectrum of N,S-CDs. The optimal ratio of CA/Meth was selected as 1:1 for the preparation of N,S-CDs. The prepared N, S-CDs's quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 32.5% higher than undoped CDs produced by hydrothermal synthesis methods. The high quantum yield of N,S-CDs could be attributed to the presence of more C–N and C–S bonds in the carbon lattice of quantum dots [35]. The reaction temperature during the preparation process appears to affect N,S-CDs' fluorescence, as illustrated in Fig. S2. The increase in reaction temperature resulted in a slight increase in the fluorescence and a trivial shifting in the emission wavelength. As far as possible, the lower temperature was chosen that does not affect the fluorescence; therefore, 180 °C was selected for subsequent experiments. For N,S-CDs synthesis, the optimal reaction time was chosen as 6 h (Fig. S3).

The absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs exhibited two peaks at 240 nm and 330 nm, indicating the π–π∗ and n–π∗ transition of the aromatic core structure and carbonyl group correspondingly (Fig. S4). Upon excitation at 370 nm, N,S-CDs emit a blue emission at 440 nm (Fig. S5). The fluorescence response of N,S-CDs is mostly influenced by the excitation wavelength (Fig. S6). Bathochromic shifts in the fluorescence emission were observed as the excitation wavelength changes from 320 to 370. This shifting might be due to CDs' quantum confinement or different size distribution of nanoscale carbon material [35]. Based on these findings, the 370 nm excitation wavelength was chosen for further experiments as it produces a sharp emission band. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of N,S-CDs are selected as 370 and 440 nm. The effect of dilution on the Fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs (0.2 g/5 mL) was investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs significantly increases with the dilution. N,S-CDs show concentration-dependent emission attributed to the change in surface states resulting from the enhanced particle aggregation in concentrated solutions [37]. Furthermore, the synthesized N,S-CDs based probe exhibits good fluorescence performance even after three months of storage at 4ᵒC. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs based probe remains nearly constant over three months. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity was unaffected by UV light irradiation for 180 min. These findings suggest that the N,S-CDs based probe has excellent potential for practical applications due to its good photostability and chemical stability.

The N,S-CDs were characterized using various spectroscopic techniques. The FTIR spectrum of as-prepared N,S-CDs (Fig. S8) revealed a characteristic band around 3422 cm−1, most likely caused by the OH-/NH- functional group on the surface of N,S-CDs [29]. The absorption band around 2917 cm−1 can be allotted to the C–H-stretching vibration. The peak around 2625 cm−1 is ascribed to S–H stretching vibrations. Additionally, the presence of the CO group is indicated by a strong band at 1705 cm−1. The cyclic CC functionality of CDs is attributed to the absorption band around 1590 and 1442 Cm-1. Furthermore, the bands for C–N, C–O, and C–S were also visible at 1336, 1275, and 1120 cm−1, respectively are also appeared in the FTIR spectrum.

The average diameter and surface morphology of N,S-CDs were estimated using HR-TEM (Fig. 1 A). N,S-CDs are sphere-shaped and homogeneously dispersed, with no deceptive accumulation. The diameter of the monodispersed N,S-CDs were calculated from morphologies of around 50 dots taken arbitrarily. The size distribution of CDs was 3.2–6.8 nm, with an average size of 4.6 nm (Fig. 1B). The (102) facet of graphite is visible in Fig. 1C, which has a crystal-like arrangement with an interlayer lattice fringe of 0.21 nm. Further selected areas electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CDs as illustrated in Fig. 1D shows diffuse rings signifying the N,S-CDs polycrystalline nature.

The XPS analysis was performed to examine the elemental composition and chemical bonds of N,S-CDs. In the XPS spectra of N, S-CDs, four prominent bands appeared at 284.8, 400.84, 530.89, and 163.1 eV, corresponding to the C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p [36] (Fig. 2 A). The relative content of elements was approximately 63.68% C, 9.25% N, 18.64% O, and 8.43% S as depicted in Fig. 2B. C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) can be integrated into three major component bands at 284.80, 285.95, and 287.93 eV, parallel to the bonding structure of CC, CO, and CN, correspondingly [38]. O1s spectrum is fitted with two prominent bands at 530.79 and 531.85 eV, indicating the existence of hydroxyl (OH) and carbonyl CO (Fig. 2D) [39]. The N1s spectra have two bands at 399.06 and 400.87 eV, respectively matching pyridine type N and pyrrolic type –N (Fig. 2E) [40]. The peaks in the S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2F) around 162.6 eV, 163.6 eV, and 166.0 eV revealed different forms of sulfur. The band at 163.05 eV and 164.15 eV are possibly due to the thiophene -C-S bond [ [35]]. The band around 165.65 eV is possibly due to –C-SO3 like functionality. The FTIR and XPS spectra revealed that N,S-CDs comprised functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur atoms; hence, the carbon dots show remarkable water solubility, superb dispersity, and desirable optical characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on optical properties of N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A suitable pH is essential for a fluorescence probe due to protonation and deprotonation [41]. Experiments were carried out under various pH values to determine the effect of pH change on N,S-CDs fluorescence. The pH of N,S-CDs was adjusted by adding 0.1 M NaOH and HCl aqueous solution, and alterations in the fluorescence intensity were logged. The fluorescence of CDs decreases with decreasing the acidity (pH = 2–5) as depicted in Fig. 3 A. This reduction in the fluorescence response may be due to protonation-induced aggregation of –COOH and –OH groups of CDs [42]. However, remarkable changes in fluorescence response were noted as the pH value of CDs increased from pH 8–12. The increase in the fluorescence intensity in the basic medium could be due to –COOH and –OH functional groups' deprotonation. The N,S-CDs show high sensitivity towards change in pH value. Therefore, the sensor's optimal pH was set at pH 7.0 for further fluorescence analysis. Fig. 3B showed the change in the fluorescence upon changing the pH reversibly in a highly acidic and basic medium.

N,S-CDs' sensing potential towards metal cations, anions, and neutral analytes was explored using fluorescence spectroscopy. For sensing experiments, 1 mL aqueous solution of various metal cations (100 μM) was treated with N,S-CDs in PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.0), and alteration in fluorescence intensity of CDs was recorded. It is clear from Fig. 4 A that fluorescence intensity remains similar upon addition of most of the metal cations except Hg2+ which produces significant quenching in the fluorescence of N,S-CDs suggesting that N,S-CDs have high selectivity for Hg2+. When exposed to UV (365 nm), the blue fluorescence of CDs is completely quenched instantaneously upon the accumulation of Hg2+ ions. N,S-CDs' fluorescence quenching upon interaction with Hg2+ is most probably due to non-radiative electron annihilation through the operative electron process. The presence of sulfur and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon dots may explain N,S-CDs' selective response towards Hg2+. The complexation reaction between the Hg2+ and sulfur and oxygen group on the surface of CDs is more substantial than other cations and generated a new complex that causes significant quenching in the fluorescence. Besides metal cations, N,S-CDs' interaction with various anions and neutral analytes was also explored (Figs. S9 and S10). The fluorescence response remains similar in most cases; only a slight enhancement in the fluorescence was recorded upon adding carbonate and phosphate ions. These results exhibit that N,S-CDs have unique binding sites for Hg2+ and can be utilized as a fluorescent probe in discerning Hg2+ ions.

Selectivity for a specific analyte is critical for the sensor's actual analytical application. To determine N,S-CDs' selectivity towards Hg2+, fluorescence response in the company of commonly interfering metal cation, anions, and other neutral analytes were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4B, metal cation and anions such as Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Pd2+, Cd2+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, NO3−, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, ClO4 − and even mixture of ions have very little or no effect on the determination of Hg2+. The fluorescence response of N,S-CDs, and Hg2+ remains similar in the company of various metal cations and anions. These findings suggest that N,S-CDs are extremely discriminatory for detecting Hg2+ even in the presence of other contaminants in the aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N,S-CDs for detection of Hg2+ and guanine
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of an N,S-CDs based probe for Hg2+ was assessed under optimized experimental conditions. Keeping the concentration of N,S-CDs constant, the concentration of Hg2+ was changed in the range of 0.05–100 μM, and alteration in fluorescence was recorded as displayed in Fig. 5 A. A steady reduction in fluorescence intensity was recorded as Hg2+ concentration rises; an excellent linear relationship was acquired between the fluorescence and Hg2+ ion concentration with R2 = 0.9929 (Fig. 5B). The detection limit was determined by utilizing the following formula LOD = 3.3 x Sd/S, where Sd represents the standard deviation for five blank measurements and S represents the slope of the straight-line equation. The limit of detection for Hg2+ was calculated to be 6.24 nM using N, S-CDs. Table 1 shows the comparison of the current method with previously reported literature for the detection of Hg2+. It can be seen from Table 1 that the current method has a high quantum yield with a low limit of detection and a wide linear range for the detection of Hg2+ ion as compared to previously reported probes.

The fluorescent turns ON response of N, S-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ was also evaluated upon the addition of various neutral analytes such as adenine, guanine, cytosine, glutathione, lysine, proline, serine, thymine, and ascorbic acid. N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity remains similar in most cases, and the only guanine gives fluorescence turn ON response in the existence of Hg2+(Fig. S11). The analytical performance of N,S-CDs@Hg2+ towards guanine detection was assessed by adding various concentrations of guanine in the range of 0.05–100 μM. It is evident from Fig. 5C that as the concentration of guanine increases, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture N,S-CDs@Hg2+/Guanine increases steadily. An excellent linear response between the fluorescence intensity and guanine concentration (0.05–100 μM) was observed with an R2 value of 0.9822. The limit of detection for guanine was calculated to be 6.4 nM (Fig. 5D).

This fluorescence turn ON-OFF-ON mechanism could be attributed to the nonradiative photoelectron transfer (PET) mechanism. Electronegativity of nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen contributes to effectively tune the overall electron density on the surface of N, S-CDs that promotes coordination of Hg2+ with surface functional groups of N,S-CDs. Therefore, fast nonradiative electron transfer to Hg2+ leads to fluorescence quenching. In the presence of guanine, a new guanine-Hg2+ interaction develops that is preferred over N, S-CDs-Hg2+ interaction resulting in the recovery of fluorescence response of N,S-CDs. This observation was further supported by visual color change under UV-light at the excitation of 365 nm. The blue color fluorescence of N,S-CDs was entirely quenched in the presence of Hg2+, and after the addition of guanine into quenched solution, fluorescence response was recovered [48].

Competitive experiments were performed company of various neutral analytes to determine the selective response of the N,S-CDs + Hg2+ system to detect guanine. N,S-CDs + Hg2+ was treated with guanine (50 μM) in the presence of other interfering analytes (50 μM), and variations in the fluorescence intensity were recorded as represented in Fig. 6 . It is seen from the figure that N,S-CDs + Hg2+ based probe is decidedly choosy for the detection of guanine even in the presence of similar nucleobases. These findings suggest that this system presents a virtuous selectivity towards guanine even in the existence of communal interfering analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the recognition of Hg2+ and guanine
ce:section.ce:para: The medium's pH plays a vital role in the formation and deformation of the complex between the sensor and the analyte. Sensing experiments were performed in a broader pH range to determine the impact of pH change on Hg2+ and guanine detection (2–12). The pH of the mixture was maintained using HCl and NaOH dilute aqueous solution. The changes in the fluorescence of N,S-CDs were recorded upon interaction with Hg2+ (under various pH values), and the graph was plotted between pH and change in the fluorescence as depicted in Fig. S12. The fluorescence of N,S-CDs decreased significantly in a broader range of pH values (2–12), demonstrating that the pH of the medium did not influence the recognition of Hg2+. Similarly, the fluorescence turn on the response of N,S-CDs + Hg2+ based probe is recovered upon accumulation of guanine in a wider range of pH (2–12). These findings suggest that the reversible fluorescence response of N,S-CDs in the attendance of Hg2+ and guanine is pH-independent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applicability of the N,S-CDs-based probe, experiments for the selective recognition of Hg2+ were performed in real water. Water samples were taken from Institute for Advanced Study (IAS) laboratory tap water and Pearl River Guangzhou, China. The obtained water samples were filtered by a 0.2-μm syringe filter and spiked with a known concentration of Hg2+ (20 nM, 40 nM, and 60 nM). The Hg2+ solution prepared in real water samples was mixed with N,S-CDs solution, and alteration in the fluorescence response was recorded (Fig. S13). These findings indicated that the components of real water samples did not affect the detection of Hg2+. Table 2 shows that the percent recovery range for Hg2+ is 94.65–103.7%, indicating that this approach is reliable and efficient for Hg2+ detection.

In summary, a fluorescent probe based on N,S-CDs has been developed by a facile hydrothermal method. The strong fluorescence response of N,S-CDs was utilized to detect various cations, anions, and neutral analytes. N,S-CDs' fluorescence response at 440 nm remains unchanged upon treatment with most analytes except Hg2+ under aqueous conditions. Hg2+ coordinated with the N,S-CDs forming an N,S-CDs@Hg2+ complex; consequently, a strong fluorescence response at 440 nm was completely quenched. Thus, a fluorescent probe with exceptional selectivity for detecting Hg2+ was developed. The completely quenched response of the N,S-CDs@Hg2+ complex was further utilized to investigate different analytes such as adenine, cytosine, guanine, thymine, lysine, serine, and serine ascorbic acid under aqueous conditions. The aqueous solution of N,S-CDs@Hg2+ complex on treatment with guanine (50 μM aqueous solution) displayed regeneration of strong fluorescence response at 440 nm. Moreover, the behavior of this probe was further explored against the change in pH at a broader range (2–12). It was observed that changes in pH do not affect the interaction N,S-CDs@Hg2+ and N,S-CDs@Hg2+/guanine complexes. In conclusion, a fluorescence probe based on an “on-off-on” mechanism was achieved for the highly selective detection of Hg2+ and guanine.

Farid Ahmed: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shahzad Iqbal: Validation, Formal analysis. Long Zhao: Software, Resources. Hai Xiong: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China (KQJSCX20180328095517269) and the NTUT-SZU Joint Research Program (Grant No. 2021003) for the research grants.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338977.
